orchestkit · #2948 · lifecycle/webhook-forwarder

The hook wasn't slow. It was spawning git.

Sessions kept printing "UserPromptSubmit hook timed out after 5s — output discarded". Idle profiling found nothing (worst hook: 1.3s). The answer was in 186,835 production timing records: every telemetry emit ran git branch --show-current synchronously in a fresh process with a 5s allowance, and under fork pressure plus index.lock contention from concurrent sessions, that single spawn ate the entire hook budget.

Production timing · ~/.claude/analytics/hook-timing.jsonl (real data, not simulated)

hook (UserPromptSubmit set)np50p95p99max>2s>4s
lifecycle/webhook-forwarder19,6211311,2593,76310,121597168
prompt/unified-dispatcher1,2181141,2832,7727,569385
prompt/thrash-detector1,169919732,6665,014232
prompt/worktree-advisory-consumer1,069825361,1475,01121
prompt/goal-tracker1,070835101,2495,00711

All 661 slow records decompose as exec-dominated (stage timings: t_exec ≈ total; bundle and stdin are noise). The two sync hooks everyone suspected show 1-2 slow events per ~1,070 — innocent. The originally-proposed fix (raise their timeouts) would have medicated the healthy patient.

One webhook-forwarder invocation · before vs after (10s scale)

Before — sync git spawn (≤5s) + 4 × 3s fetch retries, inside a 5s box
boot
git branch --show-current (blocked on index.lock)
fetch ×4 + backoff
event lost · warning printed
02.5s5s7.5s10s
After — read .git/HEAD (microseconds, no locks) + ≤3.2s bounded network, inside a 15s async box
boot
fetch ≤1.5s ×2
done
02.5s5s7.5s10s

Three changes, each at its own layer: 1) env.ts — branch comes from parsing .git/HEAD (worktree pointer files followed, detached HEAD → ''), no subprocess exists to stall; 2) http-sink.ts — in-hook network budget cut to 2 attempts × 1.5s shared abort signal (worst ≈ 3.2s), patient retries stay in SessionEnd's flush(); 3) hooks.json — webhook-forwarder (async, never blocks the prompt) raised 5s → 15s on all 38 entries, so a pathological network day degrades to "telemetry arrives late" instead of "process killed".

Why idle profiling missed it: no concurrent git operations, no lock contention, warm caches. The spawn cost ~20ms idle and up to 5,000ms under the exact conditions (vitest forks + parallel sessions) that made the timeouts fire. · #2948 · siblings: #2947, #2955
